text: This paper presents a critical discourse analysis (CDA) of the political speeches delivered by Barack Obama and Hassan Rouhani at the United Nations in September 2013. Drawing upon Halliday’s Systemic Functional Linguistics (SFL) as a theoretical framework, the study utilizes transitivity systems, modality, and personal pronouns to uncover how linguistic choices encode ideology and power. Results indicate that both leaders rely heavily on material processes to emphasize governmental action and leadership, though Obama’s discourse is characterized by more colloquial, accessible language compared to Rouhani’s formal and academic tone. Modality analysis reveals a high frequency of median-level politeness, specifically the use of "will" and "can," to project authority and future commitment. Furthermore, the strategic use of the first-person plural pronoun "we" is shown to serve the dual purpose of fostering intimacy with the audience while asserting institutional power. By examining these linguistic strategies, the study illustrates how political figures manufacture consensus and manifest their policy objectives through the "art of linguistic spin," ultimately making complex ideological positions more accessible to the general public.
